Palo Alto Networks offers exposure to the fast-growing cybersecurity market, and its growth outlook for both the current quarter and the remainder of the year is quite good. Its next-generation businesses are growing quickly, with annual recurring revenue being forecast to grow by more than 50% this quarter. Operating leverage works in the company's favor, with PANW benefiting from nice margin expansion tailwinds.

I do believe that share-based compensation remains an issue, however, and when we look at Palo Alto Networks' GAAP results, profitability isn't overly strong, despite considerable margin growth over the last year. PANW trades at around 40x this year's non-GAAP earnings, but its GAAP earnings multiple is way higher than that -- depending on where you stand on the question of how investors should treat share-based compensation expenses, you may find Palo Alto Networks quite pricey. The stock has come down in recent months and is now less expensive than a quarter ago, but that doesn't make the stock cheap -- I thus remain on the sidelines for now.

For an investor who sees PANW's share issuance to employees more like an investment than a cost, PANW could look more appealing, though. At the current price and valuation, PANW is far from a strong buy, I believe, but a potential buy for those who want growth, cyber exposure, and who do not mind the company's share dilution too much.

data.title: Horizon Technology Finance: When A 20% Yield Is Not A Bargain But A Warning
data.description:
Introduction

Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN) looks attractively inexpensive, but in reality it is cheap for a reason. Its venture-focused lending model worked in the zero-rate era, when there was a lot of capital in the market, and funders could exit easily. That environment no longer exists.

HRZN’s earnings and growth have gone down. The stock trades at roughly 0.9x book, and about 5–6x earnings. The stress is visible in the share price, which is down 30%. It is now pursuing a merger in an effort to stabilize scale and costs. However, I am skeptical that this will be enough. HRZN’s 20% yield may look attractive, but it reflects elevated structural risk rather than opportunity.

The Model That Broke For HRZN

HRZN is a BDC (a business development company) that does venture lending, offering structured debt to VC-backed technology and life sciences companies. HRZN does this at an earlier stage than traditional middle-market BDCs. These underwrite recurring cash flow. HRZN underwrites growth and the option to exit quickly. Here, repayment does not often come from cash flow but from follow-on equity raises, acquisitions, or public offerings.

There was abundant capital during the pre-2022 zero-rate period, and refinancing came easily. At that time, HRZN grew its portfolio and earnings. The past decade’s data shows this growth - revenue compounded 12.73% annually, EBIT grew at 15.74%. However, as rates rose and IPO markets faltered in 2022, venture lending as a whole suffered, and HRZN was severely impacted. In the recent past, revenue is down 5.41% YoY, net income margin is negative at -24.19%, and ROE is down 7.27%. Projections are even more dire, with forward EPS projections down 19.51%.

This is not short term volatility. The problem is more structural, and reflects a lending model exposed to a tighter capital environment that does not show any indication of being temporary.

Dividend Pressure, Coverage, And Balance Sheet Strain

HRZN’s forward dividend yield is an attractive-looking 20.47%. However, the payout ratio of 116.81% is concerning - a BDC that pays more than it earns is not operating from a position of strength. That is why dividend safety is graded D+, and consistency is C-. Those are not reassuring for an income vehicle selling itself on yield.

In Q3, management reported net investment income of $0.32 per share, modestly covering the $0.11 monthly distribution for the time being. However, the coverage cushion, at ~97%, is thin, so HRZN needs to keep charging higher interest rates to maintain earnings. However, as management itself says, that yield, at around 18.6% in Q3, is unusually high. Normalized historical yield is closer to 14.5-15%. That higher yield is a result of riskier credit, and is not sustainable. On the other hand, if the rate falls but volume still remains low due to tighter credit conditions, that would compress net interest income.

Adding to that problem is a difficult balance sheet. The net investment portfolio declined to $585 million from $636 million sequentially. Leverage is currently at 0.94x, lower than the target of 1.2-1.3x. Available liquidity stood at $151 million.

That lower leverage reduces immediate risk, but it also shows that the portfolio is contracting. Management expects originations to exceed prepayments to move leverage back toward target levels. However, that effectively increases risk exposure in a difficult venture market that remains sensitive to liquidity.

For HRZN, bad news keeps coming, with funding costs rising as well. In December, HRZN priced $57.5 million of 7.00% notes due 2028 to refinance 4.875% notes due 2026. So they are replacing sub-5% debt with 7% debt, that too in a stressed earnings environment that only increases interest expense pressure.

Take them all together - elevated payout ratio, negative profitability metrics, high, unsustainable portfolio yields, and rising funding costs—and the bear picture becomes clearer. That 20% yield does not appear as attractive as before. It doesn’t reflect excess earnings; it is simply the price the company is forced to pay investors to put their money in a higher risk asset.

The MRCC Merger - Stabilization, Not Transformation

Given these problems, HRZN is planning a merger with Monroe Capital Corporation (MRCC). The plan is to increase scale, reduce expense ratios, and improve efficiency. Management expects annual G&A savings of roughly $2.5 million, or about a 30% reduction. There will be fee waivers totaling up to $4 million in the first year after the deal is closed. These cost improvements are meaningful.

That said, the merger does not change the underlying business model - the one that was falling apart. HRZN will still remain a venture-focused lender. It will operate in the same stressed and liquidity-sensitive segment. Scale will do nothing to change those structures. It may smooth volatility, especially by making expenses a smaller percentage of the business, but it is not going to completely eliminate concentration risk. The stressed capital market conditions will also remain the same, and their effect on the company will not reduce.

In that sense, the merger appears defensive rather than transformational.

Peer Comparison: Venture Exposure vs. Diversified Scale

Among HRZN’s venture-lending peers are Hercules Capital (HTGC) and Trinity Capital (TRIN). While these are better than HRZN in many ways, they are still highly exposed to the venture scene, which is stressed right now. So if you need to invest in BDCs, you may also consider larger, diversified middle-market platforms like Ares Capital (ARCC) and Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX). Here’s a comparison table:

Here, there's a considerable difference in size, which improves scale efficiency in many ways - something that HRZN is trying to achieve with the MRCC merger. Access to deal flows, better ability to absorb quick credit losses are some of the other advantages. However, as against that, there is the problem with the venture-backed sector itself, which cannot be surmounted by just a merger.

Again, yield does not mean quality. HRZN’s portfolio yield is the highest, but that comes with a negative ROE and higher realized losses. Both ARCC and TSLX come with much lower portfolio yields, yet produce consistent double-digit ROEs and stable dividend coverage. HTGC and TRIN do show that the venture model can work, but needs better underwriting and broader origination.

The takeaway is that venture lending is not inherently flawed, but that the tight conditions of recent times, coupled with possibly better business management at some of the peers, have helped them outclass HRZN.

Upcoming Earnings: What to Watch

With earnings due next month, we need to watch for improvements in dividend coverage. NII relative to the $0.11 monthly distribution will tell us if the payout remains well-covered or we should worry about earnings slipping further away from it.

Investors need to watch for portfolio yield and leverage as well. If yields normalize but origination volume doesn’t improve, earnings could compress. More than raw growth, I would watch for the credit quality of new loans so there are fewer non-accruals.

Apart from non-accruals, funding costs and NAV movement will determine balance sheet stress. Next month’s earnings will show if HRZN may be truly turning around, or simply plowing through ongoing structural pressure.

Conclusion

HRZN hasn’t been declining because of some temporary volatility or any other resolvable cause. The decline reflects a venture-focused lending business designed to work well in a liquidity-rich environment; unfortunately for HRZN, that environment has now significantly tightened.

HRZN’s earnings have weakened, and the dividend is not well-covered by what it earns. It is forced to use higher interest rates, which elevates portfolio yield above sustainable historical norms. As rates rise, funding costs are rising. As I also noted, HRZN needs to expand risk exposure so it can restore its targets for leverage.

None of these items suggest signs of durable improvement.

At this time, the company is pursuing a merger with MRCC in the hopes of redressing the situation. It will, indeed, improve scale and cost efficiencies; however, it doesn’t change the business model, nor the problems associated with it. Current capital markets are simply not like what they were five years ago, and HRZN has not adapted its business to the new conditions.

So, a 20% dividend yield may look compelling, but it is just the price they are paying you to accept their risk. It is not something being paid because they have so much earnings. On top of that, there are much better BDCs - both the venture-focused ones, and those targeting middle and upper markets - that make far better sense than HRZN. As such, it is an avoid.

data.title: Nvidia Will Likely Beat, But The Market May Not Like It
data.description:
I was on the Investing Experts Podcast this week, and, as with any time a group of young analysts meet, we talked about Nvidia Corp. (NVDA). Not only is it still the world's largest firm after the tech sector corrected this year, although it's now below the $5T market cap mark.

It had a steep drawdown at the beginning of the month, but has snapped back and is now flat YTD.

My record on NVDA is short, as I mostly covered it through funds that give exposure to NVDA like the YieldMax NVDY ETF, having started coverage of the stock itself in July. I've maintained a buy rating through today.

It's up nearly 10% since I first recommended it, but so is the S&P 500.

Here's my rating history:

The Pressure of High Expectations

In that conversation, there were a couple of things said that I think are worth bringing up in an article directly on Nvidia, especially considering that they have their earnings report next week, on the 25th.

I'll focus on just one here. My colleague Julia Ostian said on the podcast about Nvidia's earnings:

The market looks nervous and I mean the market looks excited but worried at the same time...I just broke down Nvidia's earnings today, I wrote the preview...

It really looks like it doesn't matter what Nvidia will do. The market will not be as happy because it looks to me that the company so many times has beaten the expectations that basically right now the expectations are that everything will be better than expected.

But the problem is that this phrase can stretch basically limitless, right? What is better than expected?

...if Nvidia will deliver instead of $65 billion, $70 billion, I'm not sure the market will be very happy because Nvidia usually beats like three to four billion.

...I really think the stock can go lower on the earnings even with the [beat].

As I began to write this article, my take on NVDA earnings, I kept coming back to that line, that expectations of beating expectations can be dangerous for sentiment. Sentiment is one of the key mechanisms for earnings calls—sentiment changes bring volume, and that brings volatility. It can spiral quickly.

This isn't a new problem for NVDA, based on the last two years. We can see that when earnings estimates and reality meet or beat, they sell off just like when they miss. As some commenters have said about this phenomenon in one of my articles, “NVDA dropping on earnings is a meme.”

For reference, the “E” symbols denote an earnings call.

It has to do with these high expectations. Beats don't always impress the market, and so good news often leads to pain even if the overall trajectory is still up. While NVDA rarely saw stock gains immediately after earnings, that 2-year gain is still ~280%.

That is absolute insanity for companies this large, but the same goes for the earnings and revenue growth, which is why the market is this way about NVDA. When your EBITDA looks like this, the market has sky-high expectations, and for many traders, it's unclear what that really means.

Those who believe the meme will understand one of my past headlines: “No earnings call would've been good enough.”

NVDA Q4 Preview

As shown earlier, the Wall St. consensus for NVDA is near $70B in revenue for Q4. Corporate guidance has the headline at $65B, with gross margins at ~74%. EPS guidance is at ~$1.50, which would be a 71% YoY increase, but Wall St. consensus is even higher.

The last quarter read on gross margins was a little under target, and improvement in margins is one of the things I believe the market cares about with NVDA. I watch this figure closely, despite how high its absolute level is.

The market seems to respond to changes in it, although overlay a macro lens on this chart and maybe you don't see what I see—I can't tell if I have my blinders on here or if this looks how I think it looks.

Combine that with my ongoing push of profitability theory, the name I've given the thesis that index valuations can be tied to profitability. NVDA is the king of this class of businesses that have built very high profit margins and thus produce immense free cash flow growth.

NVDA did $22B in FCF last quarter, up ~98% YoY, with guidance at ~$25B for this quarter, unlikely but potentially a new all-time high if they overshoot—which, as mentioned before, is expected by Mr. Market.

Back to margins, I expect commentary in the earnings call itself will matter here, as investors want to hear that Blackwell chip sales are holding their margins as production scales. These are some of the newer chips that are being sold as part of the cyclical dealings that some investors are skeptical of.

Rubin may be a sore topic, as it has been a high cost with little reward yet, but that is because it is still early in the production cycle. Since NVDA is primarily a design firm and not a manufacturer, they spend a lot on R&D, especially early in the development cycle.

Critics have rightfully pointed out rising R&D costs.

But R&D is a relatively fixed cost for GPU development cycles, whereas hyperscaler capex is dynamic and now approaching $1T. As their revenue has exploded in the past two years, the ratio has dropped precipitously.

I see this increase in R&D spending by NVDA to be opportunistic, especially because even as it has doubled, its impact on bottom lines has halved. They just can afford to spend more because they make more.

In the call itself, I expect that the TPU vs. GPU conversation may come up (competition with Google (GOOG) (GOOGL)), which is really a question about sustained data center demand, or if that will fall off as they transition to a different kind of chip. I have no speculation on how Huang will answer that, but if he isn't confident and backs that up with a technical point for GPUs, that could rattle investor confidence.

One thing we can expect is volatility. Options are currently pricing in an ~11% move in either direction. It's a little crazy to consider, in liquidity terms, that a move of that magnitude means hundreds of billions made or lost for investors.

The big risk outside what I've already mentioned is that forward guidance is down. Because of all the CapEx announcements from hyperscalers hitting headlines lately, investors want to see that NVDA is capitalizing on that and providing the supply. If guidance is down, or anyone in the call mentioned spending fatigue, that could cause a sentiment panic quickly, and a sell-off, even if it rebounds the next day.

An 11% implied options move doesn't mean it will stay up or down 11% if it moves that far, but that we may see that move at a peak. Often, NVDA has rebound days following large moves, so watch out for that if you are trading around the earnings call.

Conclusion

I am maintaining my buy rating on NVDA, as it continues to be a powerhouse company in all the places that matter, like its gross margins, free cash flow, and marginal cost of development. They are an exemplary business and highly valued for good reason.

They are a firm that is often “priced to perfection” and punished for not beating estimates higher than their guidance. That's why they have a history of selling off after good earnings, as they may very well do next week.

If they do, I will be buying more shares.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Apple: Why I'm Finally Turning Bullish (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Last fall, I reiterated my hold rating on Apple Inc. (AAPL) as I cited solid results, a justified valuation, and a lack of catalysts. My neutral stance has held up well, with the stock having shown modest losses since the publication of that article. Apple's most recent earnings report a few weeks ago made headlines as iPhone sales were reported to be strong, particularly in China. Today, we'll review these results as well as discuss some noteworthy developments to see if Apple has finally become a buy in my eyes.

Below, it is shown that FY2026 Q1 was a strong quarter for Apple. Growth has accelerated with iPhone sales and the Chinese market powering their results. Looking forward, their financial guidance is solid, and for the long term, higher R&D spending combined with increased AI efforts shows that the company is moving back to its innovation roots. While there are some risks in the form of memory shortages, I would say that the strengthening momentum of their business shows there is room for multiple expansions from here. Therefore, I'm finally turning bullish on Apple stock.

Business Is Good

My first impression of Apple's top-line results is that business was good in their FY2026 Q1. Their products and services segments generated combined revenues of $143.76 billion, and that represented a strong growth rate of 15.65% YoY. You can see above that this was one of the largest accelerations in growth in the past few years and that the growth rate in the first quarter was the strongest in a long time. Therefore, Apple's overall business activity seems to be picking up and is seeing strong momentum.

It is also worth mentioning that the company scored a breathtaking beat of expectations. In Q1, their top line surpassed analyst estimates by a shocking $5.23 billion, and so Apple seems to have defied potential consumer weakness headwinds. Given the macro environment both in the U.S. and international markets, this revenue beat is one for the ages.

iPhone Stole the Show

The iPhone is, of course, their most important product, and so it is worth a closer look. For Q1, as shown above, they reported sales of $85.269 billion, and that's a 23.33% increase YoY. This is clearly a massive acceleration of growth from Q4's 6.06%, and so it seems that the latest generation of iPhones is resonating strongly with consumers. Note that iPhone was Apple's top-performing category of Q1 and it is always encouraging to see a company's flagship segment outperform. There were some concerns regarding the iPhone's competitiveness in a world of AI-powered devices, but at this point, there are no signs that this has been a major factor.

We'll touch on their overall China sales below, but earlier this month it was reported that iPhone was the only smartphone that grew sales in the market in January. China is known for a very competitive mobile device market, and so the fact that Apple's iPhone is seeing such strong results there is a testament to the attractiveness of their latest generation of devices.

China Was A Major Driver

China has long been an important market for Apple, but in Q1, growth was staggeringly impressive. Of all the geographic markets that Apple serves, China saw the largest percentage revenue increase by a huge margin. In the fiscal first quarter, they reported $25.53 billion in revenues, up 37.9%. The second fastest growing sales region was the Rest of Asia Pacific, with growth of 18%, and so China was a major source of outperformance.

What makes this particularly impressive is the fact that China's Consumer Confidence Index remains subdued. While there has been some improvement, the index remains miles below 2021 levels, and so people there are still overall reluctant to spend. Apple's explosive growth there shows that their value proposition is very strong and that they're clearly doing something very right.

Margins Improve YoY & QoQ

In addition to seeing good business, Apple is making more off of these sales. As you can see in the chart above, gross margin has expanded significantly both YoY and QoQ to show that their pricing power is as strong as ever. With significant competition, especially in China, the ability to expand gross margin is impressive.

While operating margin also improved YoY, it should be noted that the expansion was less than the 128 basis points for gross margin. For operating margin, there was a 91 basis point improvement, and so, there was a bit of operating inefficiency. However, this is not as unfavorable as it seems. If you take a look at their income statement, you can see that R&D expense increased by 32% YoY while SG&A expense increased by just 4%. In my previous article, I highlighted that an increased R&D budget may be beneficial for long-term innovation and shareholder value, and so the overall increase in operating expenses really isn't a concern.

Apple reported a diluted EPS of $2.84 and that represented a nice growth rate of 18%. This outpaced the revenue growth rate as an indication that the gross margin improvement made it all the way to the bottom line. In addition, they beat expectations by $0.17 to show outperformance as well.

Financial Outlook

Looking forward to Q2, Apple expects revenues to grow in the range of 13% to 16%. That implies that a mild deceleration may occur, but overall this is a very respectable growth rate in the grand scheme of things and indicates business will remain good. In fact, they stated that iPhone supply will be constrained, and so demand is not the issue for their flagship product. For gross margin, they are expecting this figure to be in the range of 48% to 49%. This should be an expansion from the year-ago quarter's 47%, and so the pricing power strength we have seen is set to continue. Lastly, for operating expenses, their midpoint projection of $18.55 billion represents a 21% jump. This will outpace the expected top line growth rate, but if it's R&D that's the driver of this, again, I wouldn't mind at all.

AI Efforts

An analysis of a big tech name wouldn't be complete without a discussion of their AI efforts. While Apple has been struggling to keep pace with competitors in this area, they are still trying to remain relevant. Earlier this month it was reported that Apple is planning to update CarPlay so that users can access third-party chatbots. This should help to secure Apple's position in vehicle infotainment.

Furthermore, just days ago a report came out stating that the company is developing AI glasses and a pendant, and also working to update their AirPods with AI features. This is an even clearer statement by the company that they aren't going down in the AI showdown without a fight.

It's not all good news, however, as it was reported earlier in the month that Apple has scaled back plans for an AI-based health coach service. That said, they are still looking to roll out features in their existing Health app over time, and so this is a hiccup rather than a derailment of their AI efforts, in my view.

Memory Shortage Risks

Just today, Evercore stated that Apple has seen bullish sentiment despite the memory shortage potentially affecting the hardware sector. In my view, investors should remain somewhat cautious over this issue. With AI data centers causing unprecedented demand for memory chips, supply is tight and prices are rising. For a company like Apple, whose products need memory chips, this could create some challenges. Therefore, while business activity is seeing good momentum and the demand for their latest iPhone is high, the memory chip shortage still has the potential to upend an otherwise strong year of sales. Investors should keep this in mind.

Valuation Could Expand

Apple's forward P/E ratio is notably off its highs seen in 2025. While a reading of 31.18 isn't particularly cheap, I believe the risk/reward has become net positive for investors now. In this article, it was determined that Q1 was a robust quarter for the company. Revenue growth is at multiyear highs, their latest iPhones are seeing strong performance, and the financial outlook is respectable. Furthermore, the company is continuing to boost R&D and is pushing into AI with multiple device releases incoming.

While the memory shortage is a potential risk to keep in mind, I would say that the strong momentum Apple is seeing more than justifies the valuation, and so there may actually still be room for some multiple expansion. The company hasn't seen this strong of a trajectory for an extended period of time, and with their prospects seemingly strengthening, now may not be a bad time to buy the stock.

Takeaway

As stated in the intro, I maintained my hold rating on Apple in my previous update due to a lack of catalysts. In today's analysis, I believe I found enough bullish signs to justify an upgrade to a buy rating. As stated above, revenue growth has accelerated strongly, with the latest iPhone being a major hit, particularly in China. In addition, the upcoming launches of new AI-powered devices could power growth, and so despite some risks, Apple's strengthening prospects are likely to drive upside in the stock.
